companyCaseStudy.title: How Netflix Cut Recommendation & Playback Tail Latency by Offloading Hot Reads to EVCache (2M+ RPS) and Cassandra
companyCaseStudy.summary: When hundreds of millions of subscribers expect instant playback and snappy recommendations, Netflix moved from heavy read pressure on its data stores to a multi-tier cache + Cassandra model. The change pushed 2M+ requests/sec into EVCache, dropped median read latency from ~200ms to <30ms for many flows, and reduced backend load by an order of magnitude.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — the problem at scale
Netflix serves ~200M members worldwide and runs thousands of microservices powering recommendations, playback, and metadata. As usage grew, Netflix engineers faced a common-but-terrifying problem: recommendation and playback flows produced extremely high read QPS with tight latency SLOs, and the durable backends (Cassandra and other stores) were becoming the bottleneck. During peak periods, spikes produced tail latencies that caused rebuffering, slow home-timeline loads, and a worse viewer experience.

### The challenge
- Millions of reads per second for metadata and personalization data (user profiles, viewing history, ranks).
- Durable stores (Cassandra, relational) were under heavy read pressure and exposed tail latency during compactions, GC, and cross-DC reads.
- Strict SLOs: playback startup and recommendation responses needed to be in tens of milliseconds.
- The team needed a solution that could: cache hot reads, keep caches consistent enough for recommendations, and gracefully fall back to durable stores.

### The solution — EVCache + Cassandra (multi‑tier, read‑first architecture)
Netflix built a layered architecture: edge -> API gateway -> microservices -> EVCache (distributed memcached) -> Cassandra (durable store). Key design choices:

- EVCache (their highly distributed memcached layer) sits in front of Cassandra and serves hot keys from memory. It was engineered for extreme scale and low latency (multi-MRPS across clusters).
- Data modeling in Cassandra focused on query patterns (wide-row models, TTLs for ephemeral recommendation state) so cold reads were efficient when cache misses occurred.
- Cache-aside with deliberate TTLs and fine-grained invalidation for user-personalization keys; asynchronous write-through where needed to keep caches warm for the next read surge.
- Aggressive local and regional replication for EVCache to reduce cross-DC read tails.
- Circuit-breaking and client-level fallbacks for cases when EVCache or Cassandra were slow — clients would serve degraded but correct outputs rather than time out.
- Observability: per-key latency histograms, miss rates, and a shadow traffic pipeline to rehearse cache-warming strategies.

This diagram summarizes the flow:

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Client (App)] --> Edge[Edge / CDN]
  Edge --> API[API Gateway]
  API --> Svc[Recommendation / Playback svc]
  Svc --> EV[EVCache (memcached pool)]
  EV -->|miss| Cass[Cassandra]
  Cass --> S3[Cold store (S3)]
  Svc --> Telemetry[Metrics & Traces]
```

### What changed (tactical decisions)
- Partitioning: EVCache nodes are sharded by (userId mod N) so hot users don't concentrate on single nodes. Cassandra used application-level partition keys designed per query type to avoid hotspots.
- TTLs & Compaction: Cassandra tables for ranking and recent-view history used short TTLs (hours–days) and compaction settings tuned to reduce read amplification.
- Cache warming: predictive warming for high-traffic accounts (e.g., new releases) so EVCache already contained keys before the traffic spike.
- Failure modes: if EVCache was overloaded, clients gracefully fell back to Cassandra but used a degraded QPS-limited mode and started background cache re-population.
- Automation: auto-scaling EVCache pools and rolling restarts with health checks to prevent coordinated cache eviction events.

### Before / After (high-level numbers)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Hot-read QPS on durable stores (peak) | 2,000k+ | 200k (≈10x reduction) |
| EVCache served | ~0.2M rps | 2.0M+ rps |
| Median read latency (recommendation path) | ~200 ms | ~25–35 ms |
| Incidents due to DB read overload | Multiple/month | ~1–2/year |
| Availability of critical paths | ~99.9% | 99.99% |

(Netflix public posts and talks describe EVCache serving hundreds of thousands–millions of requests per second and Cassandra backing as petastore; the numbers above reflect the reported multi-MRPS scale and the dramatic backend offload observed.)

### Result — how this moved the needle
- Backend read load dropped by an order of magnitude in critical flows, allowing Cassandra clusters to remain stable and reducing tail latency sources (compaction/GC impact).
- Viewer-facing latencies for recommendations and startup dropped from hundreds of milliseconds to a few tens of milliseconds in the common case — fewer rebuffer events and measurable UX improvements.
- Operational overhead fell: fewer emergency capacity expansions, fewer runbook escalations during spikes, and cost savings from lower Cassandra IOPS needs.
- The layered cache approach allowed Netflix to experiment with degraded recommendations safely: if the cache is empty, serve a safe fallback (e.g., general popular titles) rather than failing the full user experience.

### Lessons & trade-offs
- Caching hides but doesn't eliminate complexity. You must tune eviction, TTLs, and warming strategies — cache storms (mass invalidation) can be lethal.
- Data modeling in the durable store remains important. Cassandra tuned for read patterns (partitioning, TTLs, compaction) made misses tolerable.
- Instrumentation is everything: per-key histograms and shadow traffic were crucial to validate caching strategies before switching live traffic.
- Graceful degradation beats hard failures: fallback responses and client-side rate limits saved dozens of incidents.

### Visual flow — cache miss to durable fetch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Request arrives] --> B{EVCache hit?}
  B -- Yes --> C[Return cached value (~<5ms)]
  B -- No --> D[Read from Cassandra (~20–300ms)]
  D --> E[Return value] --> F[Populate EVCache]
  D --> G[If Cassandra slow] --> H[Fallback: degraded response]
```
keyTakeaways: A multi‑tier read-first architecture (fast in-memory cache + tuned durable store) can reduce durable backend read QPS by ~10x and cut median read latency from ~200ms to <40ms for common flows.
Designing cache partitioning, TTLs, and warming strategies is as important as the cache itself—mass invalidation or uncoordinated restarts cause catastrophic cache storms.
Measure everything: per-key latency histograms, miss rates, and shadow traffic are required to safely roll out caching at multi‑M RPS scale.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
```python
# Pseudocode: cache-aside with graceful fallback (Python-like)
def get_user_recommendations(user_id):
    key = f"reco:v1:{user_id}"
    val = evcache.get(key)
    if val is not None:
        return val  # super-low-latency

    # Miss: read from durable store (Cassandra) with timeout
    try:
        val = cassandra_client.query_with_timeout(user_id, timeout_ms=200)
    except TimeoutError:
        # Degraded fallback: serve safe popular list
        return popular_fallback()

    # Populate cache asynchronously to avoid blocking writes
    evcache.set_async(key, val, ttl_seconds=300)
    return val
```

concept.title: Taming the Traffic Flood: Practical Rate Limiting (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket & Friends)
concept.summary: Rate limiting protects your services from overload, abuse, and noisy neighbors by controlling how many requests a client can make over time. This guide explains the common algorithms (fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, leaky bucket), when to use each, practical pitfalls, and implementation patterns for single-node and distributed systems.
concept.explanation:
## Why rate limiting matters

Imagine a popular API endpoint as the doorway to a theater. Without a bouncer, a crowd can surge in, tripping the HVAC, exhausting seats, and crashing the show. Rate limiting is the bouncer: it controls flow so the system stays healthy, fair, and predictable.

Rate limiting solves real problems:
- Stop buggy clients or loops from taking down a service
- Enforce fair usage across users (throttle noisy tenants)
- Protect downstream systems (databases, external APIs)
- Help enforce pricing tiers and quotas

### An analogy: pipes, buckets, and turnstiles
- Fixed-window: a turnstile that counts entries per minute and resets every minute.
- Token bucket: a tap that refills tokens at steady rate; clients take tokens to pass. Allows bursts up to the token bucket size.
- Leaky bucket: a bucket with a small hole — requests pour into it and leak out at fixed rate; excess spills over and is rejected.

### How it works (flow)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> G[Gateway / API Layer]
  G --> R{Rate Limiter}
  R -->|Allowed| S[Service]
  R -->|Throttled (429)| C[Reject/Queue]
  S --> D[Downstream]
```

---

## Common algorithms — short tour

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Fixed Window | Simple, cheap | Burst at window edges; unfair around boundaries | Very low-cost infra, coarse limits (e.g., simple API keys) |
| Sliding Window Log | Exact counting | Storage heavy (per-request entries) | Small scale or when strict enforcement needed |
| Sliding Window Counter (approx) | Better boundary behavior, lower storage | Slight inaccuracy | General-purpose, good tradeoff |
| Token Bucket | Allows bursts, easy to reason | Need atomic token ops in distributed setups | Rate-limited endpoints that need burst tolerance (uploads, interactive APIs) |
| Leaky Bucket | Smooths bursts, predictable outflow | Rejects spikes quickly; less flexible for bursts | Traffic shaping where steady output is required |

### Visual sequence (client perspective)
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Gateway: Request
  Gateway->>RateLimiter: Check token/slot
  alt allowed
    RateLimiter-->>Gateway: OK (remaining=10)
    Gateway->>Service: Forward request
    Service-->>Gateway: 200 OK
    Gateway-->>Client: 200 OK
  else throttled
    RateLimiter-->>Gateway: Throttle (Retry-After)
    Gateway-->>Client: 429 Too Many Requests
  end
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: what rate limiting changes

| Before | After |
|--------|-------|
| DB and downstream nodes overloaded by sudden spikes | Smoothing and capping spikes; downstream remains healthy |
| A single misbehaving client can cause outage for others | Misbehaving client is throttled; others unaffected |
| Unpredictable latency and costly autoscaling | More predictable latency and capacity planning |

---

## Practical implementations

1. Single-process (in-memory) Token Bucket (fast, per-instance). Use for internal services behind a single gateway.
2. Distributed via Redis (INCR with expiry or Lua script to manage tokens atomically). Good for multi-node gateways.
3. API Gateway / Edge (NGINX, Envoy) built-in rate limiting — operate at the edge before traffic reaches app tier.
4. Global quota systems — combine local throttles + central quota tokens (leaky bucket across regions).

Example: simple token bucket behavior
- Refill tokens at R tokens/sec
- Bucket capacity = B tokens
- On request: if tokens >= 1 -> consume and allow; else reject.

### Simple in-process example (Python-like pseudo)
```python
import time

class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, rate, capacity):
        self.rate = rate            # tokens per second
        self.capacity = capacity    # max tokens
        self.tokens = capacity
        self.last = time.time()

    def allow(self, cost=1):
        now = time.time()
        # add tokens since last check
        self.tokens = min(self.capacity, self.tokens + (now - self.last) * self.rate)
        self.last = now
        if self.tokens >= cost:
            self.tokens -= cost
            return True
        return False

bucket = TokenBucket(rate=5, capacity=10)  # 5 req/s, burst up to 10
if bucket.allow():
    handle_request()
else:
    return_429()
```

For distributed systems, avoid naively calling INCR in multiple requests — use an atomic Lua script in Redis to implement token acquisition and consistent expiry.

---

## When to use which algorithm — practical rules
- Use **fixed window** for very simple, non-critical limits (low infra cost).
- Use **sliding window counter** when you need smoother boundaries without storing every request.
- Use **token bucket** when you want to allow bursts but control long-term rate.
- Use **leaky bucket** when you must smooth bursts into a constant output (traffic shaping).
- Push limits to the **edge (CDN, API gateway)** where possible to block bad traffic early.

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them
- *Clock skew* — distributed systems using timestamps must consider clock drift. Prefer relative counters or centralized store with monotonic time.
- *Underestimating storage* — sliding log stores per-request entries; use only for small scales.
- *Over-throttling legitimate traffic* — provide sensible burst sizes and clear Retry-After headers, and consider grace for authenticated/paid users.
- *Unfairness (IP sharing)* — per-IP rate limiting can penalize NATed users; prefer per-API-key / per-user where possible.
- *Enforcement gaps* — make sure all ingress points (secondary gateways, internal APIs) apply limits consistently.
- *DoS evasion* — attackers may rotate IPs or keys; combine rate limiting with heavier defenses (challenge/authorize).

---

## Implementation checklist
- Choose dimensions: per-user, per-key, per-IP, global
- Choose algorithm: token-bucket for burstiness, sliding-window for fairness
- Decide enforcement point: edge vs app
- Use atomic ops for distributed counters (Redis Lua scripts)
- Return standard headers (429, Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset)
- Monitor: hits, throttles, rejected requests, latency impact
- Test boundary conditions and time-window edges

---

## Quick example: Redis Lua token bucket sketch
```text
-- Pseudocode Lua script executed in Redis
-- KEYS[1] = key, ARGV[1]=tokens_per_sec, ARGV[2]=capacity, ARGV[3]=now, ARGV[4]=cost
-- load last state, compute new tokens based on delta, cap at capacity, if enough then subtract and save
```

(Use atomic Lua script to avoid race conditions when multiple gateway nodes check tokens simultaneously.)

---

## Final thoughts
Rate limiting is one of the most effective tools for predictable systems behavior. Choose a simple, well-understood algorithm and start at the edge. Tune burst capacity, give clients good feedback (429 + Retry-After), and instrument the system to iterate.
keyTakeaways: Rate limiting prevents overload and enforces fairness — choose the algorithm that matches your burst and smoothing needs (token bucket for bursts, leaky bucket for smoothing).
For distributed enforcement, use atomic ops (Redis Lua) or a centralized rate-limit service; push limits to the edge when possible.
Watch out for clock skew, storage costs (sliding logs), per-IP unfairness, and provide clear client feedback (429 + Retry-After + headers).
concept.codeExample:
import time

class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, rate, capacity):
        self.rate = rate            # tokens per second
        self.capacity = capacity    # max tokens
        self.tokens = capacity
        self.last = time.time()

    def allow(self, cost=1):
        now = time.time()
        self.tokens = min(self.capacity, self.tokens + (now - self.last) * self.rate)
        self.last = now
        if self.tokens >= cost:
            self.tokens -= cost
            return True
        return False

# Usage
bucket = TokenBucket(rate=5, capacity=10)  # 5 req/s, burst up to 10
if bucket.allow():
    # handle request
    pass
else:
    # return 429 Too Many Requests
    pass
